notes.note: The Roman name for southern Greece (the Peloponnese and Attica). Cicero shunned it because it harboured exiled Catilinarian enemies, which is why he avoided Epirus and Athens.
The port on the Illyrian coast (modern Durres) where the Via Egnatia began. The normal landfall for travellers crossing from Brundisium toward Macedonia.
The great port-city of the province of Asia, point of departure for ships to Greece. Quintus Cicero was returning from his governorship of Asia and sailed from here.
A freedman in Cicero's service acting as a courier during the exile, sent to intercept Quintus. Driven off course by weather, he failed to meet him and reached Cicero at Pella.
Troy, on the Asian coast near the Hellespont. Phaethon's ship was blown back from here, delaying his crossing into Macedonia.
The old Macedonian royal capital, on the road inland from the coast toward Thessalonica. Here Phaethon finally caught up with Cicero.
Idus Maiae, i.e. 15 May 58 BC. The Ides fell on the 15th in March, May, July and October, and on the 13th in other months.
An agent or freedman (the name suggests a freedman of the Caecilii) whom Atticus had evidently sent or mentioned. Cicero notes only that he has not seen him; the man is otherwise unknown.

Tigranes the Younger, the captive Armenian prince whom Clodius had freed for a bribe, to Pompey's anger. Cicero means that since even this affront failed to provoke a breach, there is no real political shift to hope for.
Publius Plautius Hypsaeus, a close lieutenant of Pompey. Cicero thanks him and Varro as intermediaries who had spoken to Pompey on his behalf.

notes.anchor: Sent the fourth day before the Kalends of June
notes.note: Ante diem IV Kalendas Iunias, i.e. 29 May 58 BC. The first dated letter Cicero sent from Thessalonica.
